exemplar.title: To what extent can the Bayesian model of statistics be used to accurately model or predict different biases within the field of criminal justice and how does it compare to the frequentist approach to statistics?

detailedExemplarData.title: To what extent can the Bayesian model of statistics be used to accurately model or predict different biases within the field of criminal justice and how does it compare to the frequentist approach to statistics?

subject.title: Mathematics Analysis and Approaches (AA)
subject.description:
**IB Mathematics Analysis and Approaches** is a **Group 5** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **number and algebra, functions, geometry and trigonometry, statistics and probability, and calculus**, with a strong focus on **theoretical understanding, formal mathematical reasoning, and analytical problem-solving**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2021**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Non-calculator written exam | 90 minutes | 120 minutes | 40% |
| **Paper 2** | Calculator allowed written exam | 90 minutes | 120 minutes | 40% |
| **Paper 3** | Calculator allowed problem-solving | N/A | 60 minutes | 20% (HL only) |
| **Mathematical Exploration** | Internal Assessment | 20 hours | 20 hours | 20% |

strengths: The report communicates its focus on contrasting Bayesian and frequentist methods clearly in each major section, with an explicit statement of purpose.
The research question is presented prominently on the title page and revisited throughout, ensuring the reader understands the investigation.
The methodology outlines appropriate data sources, software (JASP) and test procedures, showing a generally coherent plan.
weaknesses: The compound research question diffuses focus, and occasional drift into general commentary interrupts the precision of the inquiry.
Key methodological justificationsâprior selection, sampling representativeness and limitationsâare sketched but not fully developed.
A few narrative transitions are incomplete, which slightly disrupts the roadmap of the investigation.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student delivers a consistently articulated focus and generally appropriate methods, but small lapses in question precision and methodological justification keep the work at 4/6. This aligns with the 3â4 band descriptors: the purpose and focus are clear but not sustained at the highest level, and the methodology, while mostly complete, lacks full rigor.

strengths: Subject-specific terminology (Bayes factor, posterior, p-value, chi-square) is used correctly to support explanations.
Visual aids (inferential plots, Beta distribution figures) are well integrated, reinforcing conceptual points.
Key formulae are typeset accurately, aiding reader comprehension.
weaknesses: Occasional conceptual errors persist (confusing Bernoulli likelihood with Beta prior, swapping roles of P(Data) and P(Î¸)).
Rationale for prior choices and sensitivity checks are noted but not fully justified.
Informal phrasings and incomplete bibliographic entries detract from the scholarly tone.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The work demonstrates a good grasp of central statistical concepts and applies them to the context, but recurring minor inaccuracies and gaps in justification limit this criterion to 3/6, consistent with the 1â2 to 3â4 band boundary.

strengths: The student assembles a broad range of primary data and scholarly sources, with purposeful selection tied to the research question.
Interpretations of Bayes factors and p-values are generally clear and linked to the question of bias.
The argument is structured logically, with an explicit counterargument and discussion of limitations.
weaknesses: Evaluation of alternative explanations (confounders, data quality) is only superficial.
Some analytical conclusions overreach (e.g., asserting impossibility of frequentist quantification without acknowledging confidence-interval methods).
Depth of evaluation into prior sensitivity and effect-size uncertainty is limited, reducing critical depth.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Evidence of good research relevance and clear reasoning places the work in the 4â6 band, but the lack of consistently critical evaluation and depth of analytical scrutiny caps the score at 5/12.

strengths: The overall structure (table of contents, numbered sections, conclusion, appendix) follows IB conventions and supports reader navigation.
Mathematical notation and figures are generally legible and placed appropriately.
weaknesses: Page numbering and section ordering contain mistakes (out-of-sequence entries in the table of contents).
Citation formatting is inconsistent, and several URLs are broken.
Headings vary in style, and a few layout elements (figure resolution, capitalization) lack uniformity.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: While the reportâs structure is clear, multiple formatting and layout inconsistencies limit its presentation quality to the lower half of the 1â2 band, meriting 2/4.

strandSelections.reasoning: The research question is clearly stated on the title page and repeated within the text, but its compound structure (two verbs â âmodel or predictâ and âcompareâ) leads to partial rather than tight focus.  Throughout the essay the student often emphasises one component (Bayesian-vs-frequentist comparison) more strongly than the other (degree of accuracy in modelling bias), so the connection between the RQ and every subsequent discussion is not always seamless.  These features align with the 3-4 band: clarity is achieved, yet complete sharpness and sustained focus are not.

The essay conducts two main statistical investigations, reporting Bayes factors, p-values, and contingency analyses.  The interpretations show **clear reasoning** and connect to the notion of bias.  However, effect sizes, uncertainty, and sensitivity to prior choice are treated only superficially; some conclusions (e.g., âno way to quantifyâ under frequentism) overreach.  The analysis is therefore mostly supported but not consistently rigorous, fitting comfortably in the 4-6 band with a score of 5.

Key termsâBayes factor, prior, posterior, p-value, chi-square, Beta distributionâare used frequently and generally accurately, supporting reader understanding.  Symbols are defined and formulae correctly typeset.  Nevertheless, sporadic errors (e.g., confusing Bernoulli with Beta, minor degree-of-freedom slip) and some informal phrasings (âreally difficultâ, âstuffâ) prevent full consistency.  Overall accuracy aligns with the middle band, top of 3-4, so a score of 4 is appropriate.

Formatting conventionsâtitle, word count, page numbers, tables and figuresâare mostly met, and mathematical notation is clear.  Citations use a recognisable style, though some URLs are broken and reference entries are inconsistently formatted.  A few figures are low-resolution and some headings vary in capitalization.  These lapses show that layout considerations are applied but not entirely consistent, placing the work in the lower half of the 3-4 band; a score of 3 reflects these moderate shortcomings.

The essay demonstrates **good grasp of Bayesian and frequentist concepts**: Bayesâ theorem, priors, likelihoods, Bayes factors, chi-square tests and p-values are correctly described and applied to real data.  Calculations are largely facilitated by software, and occasional misconceptions arise (e.g., describing the Bernoulli as a Beta distribution, minor mis-statements about parameters as ârandom constantsâ).  Despite these inaccuracies, the mathematical reasoning is mostly correct and linked to the context, fitting the 3-4 band with a solid score of 4.

The student introduces the theme of contrasting Bayesian and frequentist statistics in criminal-justice contexts in the opening paragraph and reiterates this focus in every major section.  The purposeâshowing that Bayesian methods âare more accurate at modelling biasesââis explicit and threads through the structure, culminating in Section 4âs synthesis.  Minor issues remain: the framing sometimes drifts into general commentary and the long, double-barrelled wording of the research question occasionally diffuses the focus.  Nevertheless, the reader is rarely in doubt about what is being investigated or why it matters, satisfying the top-band descriptors, albeit with small lapses that justify a score of 5 rather than 6.

The report follows a **logical, IB-appropriate structure**: title page, abstract-style introduction, numbered sections, conclusion, references, and extensive appendix.  Headings, sub-headings and a detailed table of contents guide the reader effectively.  Minor ordering slips (e.g., page numbers mis-sequenced) and some redundancy in section placement slightly impede flow, but overall the structure clearly supports comprehension, justifying the top 3-4 band with a score of 4.

The student collates a substantial body of primary data (exoneration registry) and a range of scholarly sources on Bayesian statistics.  Citations generally support the analysis, and the choice of counties and variables is explained in relation to the RQ, showing **good relevance and purposeful selection**.  Some sources are not critically appraised and a few web links are incomplete, limiting the evidence of *consistent* excellence, hence the lower end of the 7-9 band (score 7).

The discussion contrasts Bayesian and frequentist outcomes and offers a **reasoned argument** about applicability in criminal-justice contexts, including an explicit counterargument section.  Evaluation of limitations (e.g., subjectivity of priors) is present but not deeply critical; alternative explanations (data quality, confounders) receive limited scrutiny.  The argumentâs structure is coherent yet occasionally repetitive, aligning with the middle 4-6 band; a score of 5 reflects sound but not fully critical evaluation.

The student outlines data sources (National Registry of Exonerations) and explains the choice of two counties plus a raceâexoneration contingency table.  Software (JASP) is named, and procedures for Bayesian and frequentist tests are sketched.  However, justification for sampling decisions, prior selection, and the limitations of using population parameters in a one-sample Bayes t-test remain only briefly treated.  Alternative data or triangulation is absent and some methodological claims (e.g., impossibility of frequentist comparison) are overstated.  This reflects a mostly complete but not fully rigorous methodology, meriting the 3-4 band with a score of 4.

annotations.comment: The table of contents lists Section 4.2 on page 24 and Section 4.1 on page 25, which is out of sequence. Reorder entries to match the actual flow and avoid reader confusion.
Headings in the contents vary in capitalization and formatting style. Standardize capitalization and bolding to improve consistency and professionalism.
The Bayesâ theorem is correctly stated, but the text should clarify the conditional nature of priors and likelihoods. For example, define each term in context of Î¸ and Data for consistency.
The posterior formula is correct, but the description swaps the roles of P(Data) and P(Î¸). Ensure each termâs definition aligns with standard notation.
No rationale is provided for the prior choice in JASP. Justify the default prior parameters or explore sensitivity to alternative priors.
The inferential plots and robustness check provide a thorough view of how Bayes Factor varies with prior width, demonstrating good analytical awareness.
The policyâscenario narrative is engaging but ends abruptly. Complete the hypothetical to fully illustrate how Bayes Factor informs decisionâmaking.
Strong explanation of Beta distributions and clear figure integration demonstrate effective use of statistical concepts and visual aids.
Although the word count is provided, the investigation lacks a clear, standalone title at the start. Add a concise title to meet IB formatting requirements and guide the reader.

The assertion that âthereâs no way to quantify the differenceâ under frequentism is incorrect; a two-sample proportion test or confidence interval could assess significance. Acknowledge these options or justify why they were not used.
The historical example is strong but would benefit from critical evaluation of alternative explanations, such as sample size limitations or external confounders.
The description of the p-value process conflates likelihood with posterior probability. Clarify that the p-value assesses data extremity under Hâ and does not yield Pr(Hâ|data).
The claim that frequentist methods âcannot be appliedâ to population parameters is misleading; consider discussing oneâsample tests or appropriate null distributions to enhance accuracy.

The research questionâs dual focus (âmodel or predictâ vs. âcompareâ) diffuses its precision. Consider rephrasing to isolate one main inquiry and ensure each section consistently addresses that specific aim.

The introduction effectively situates the controversy around statistical methods in criminal justice and clearly motivates a comparison of Bayesian and frequentist approaches, demonstrating solid subject knowledge.
The claim that Bayesian inference has a âlack of assumptionsâ oversimplifies both paradigms. Provide a more nuanced critique by identifying specific assumptions in each framework.
This paragraph ends mid-sentence, disrupting the narrative flow. Ensure all transitions are complete to maintain clear communication of the methodological roadmap.
Reference citations to SCOCAL lack a year and full title. Provide complete bibliographic details for legal cases to meet academic standards.
The noninformative prior example is clear, but the text should explain why Beta(1,1) parallels frequentist flat priors and its implications for interpreting results.
The methodology overview remains abstract. Specify the actual dataset sizes, selection criteria, and any data cleaning steps to demonstrate a rigorous plan.
The critique of frequentist tests is generic. Strengthen analysis by contrasting specific cases where p-values mislead, supported by references to misinterpretation studies.
The figure is informative but lacks commentary on how choices of Î± and Î² reflect specific prior beliefs in the criminal-justice context. Connect parameter values to real-world beliefs.
The right-skewed Beta(3,6) is noted but lacks mathematical explanation. A brief remark on the mean and mode shift would deepen understanding.
Degrees of freedom are calculated correctly, but discuss whether a continuity correction or exact test might be prudent given cell counts.
Discussion of noninformative vs. informative priors is relevant, but the link to the research question (bias detection) should be explicitly restated here.
The Bernoulli likelihood is stated correctly, but the description conflates Bernoulli with Beta distributions. Clarify that the Beta is the conjugate prior, not the likelihood function.
Presenting the integral is useful, but the student should at least outline how it simplifies to the Beta function to demonstrate analytical depth.
The Bayes Factor thresholds are introduced without citation; include the Jeffreys scale or relevant source to support these interpretive benchmarks.
The null hypothesis Hâ: PââPâ=0 treats exoneration rates as population means, but these rates are sample estimates. Clarify the sampling distribution assumptions.
Justification for focusing on Cook and Harris counties is clear, but further explanation of sampling representativeness and potential selection bias is needed.
The frequency table is wellâconstructed and meets chiâsquare requirements, demonstrating clear understanding of categorical data presentation.
The Bayesian contingencyâtable results are clear, yet the narrative omits explicit interpretation of BFââ=0.455. Link this value to evidence strength statements.
Showing BFââ increases with prior concentration is valuable; however, explain why concentrations of 1, 10, and 100 were chosen and their substantive meaning.
The conclusion cogently summarizes the comparative advantages of Bayesian methods in criminal justice, reinforcing the essayâs coherent structure.
Steinhardt (2019) citation appears incomplete and lacks publication details. Ensure full reference formatting in the bibliography.
References use inconsistent styles (e.g., italicization, URL formats). Apply a uniform citation style throughout.
Several entries include redirect URLs (Google.com). Replace them with direct links to original sources to improve accessibility and credibility.
